perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is made subject to, and shall be construed under, the laws of the State of Illinois.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is made subject to, and shall be construed under, the laws of a jurisdiction of MyPoints.com's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: This clause originally specifies Illinois law governs the contract. By changing it to a jurisdiction of MyPoints.com's choosing, it allows them to potentially select a jurisdiction with laws unfavorable to the Receiving Party, potentially violating due process or fair contract principles. Illinois has laws regarding fair business practices and consumer protection, and this change allows those to be circumvented.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act protects consumers from unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices. By allowing MyPoints.com to choose any jurisdiction's law, the contract could potentially sidestep these protections, violating the Act.

( ) 25 ILCS 40/0.01 (25 ILCS 40/0.01) (from Ch. 63, par. 330) Sec. 0.01. Short title. This Act may be cited as the Governor's Bill Action Report Act. (Source: P.A. 86-1324.) 25 ILCS 40/1 (25 ILCS 40/1) (from Ch. 63, par. 331) Sec. 1. The Governor shall report any action taken by him pursuant to paragraph (b), (d) or (e) of Section 9 of Article IV of the Constitution on any bill passed by the General Assembly to the Clerk of the House of Representatives and the Secretary of the Senate within 2 days after taking such action. The report shall specify the action taken and shall include a copy of the message from the Governor to the house of origin pertaining to the bill. The Clerk of the House of Representatives and the Secretary of the Senate shall promptly distribute copies of such report and message to the members of the House of Representatives and Senate, respectively. (Source: P.A. 77-1694 .) 25 ILCS 40/2 (25 ILCS 40/2) (from Ch. 63, par. 332) Sec. 2. This Act does not affect the time allowed the respective houses of the General Assembly to take action with respect to the Governor's action on any bill as set forth in paragraph (c) of Section 9 of Article IV of the Constitution. (Source: P.A. 77-1694 .) Top Home | Legislation & Laws | House | Senate | My Legislation | Disclaimers | Email This site is maintained for the Illinois General Assembly by the Legislative Information System, 705 Stratton Building, Springfield, Illinois 62706 Contact ILGA Webmaster
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Illinois law. The changed text allows MyPoints.com to choose the governing law, potentially circumventing Illinois consumer protection laws. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate the Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly relevant as it concerns the Governor's Bill Action Report Act, the absence of specific counter-evidence in the scraped snippets does not negate the potential for contradiction highlighted by the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, which point towards a circumvention of consumer protection laws. The change directly alters a core legal obligation, making the contract potentially unenforceable in Illinois, thus creating a direct and impactful contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that Receiving Party becomes legally compelled to disclose any of the Confidential Information, Receiving Party shall provide MyPoints.com with prompt notice so that it may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Receiving Party becomes legally compelled to disclose any of the Confidential Information, Receiving Party shall inform MyPoints.com.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires 'prompt notice' and allows MyPoints.com to seek a protective order. Removing the 'prompt notice' and the protective order provision puts the Receiving Party at legal risk. Illinois law recognizes the attorney-client privilege and trade secret protection, and this change weakens the Receiving Party's ability to protect privileged information, potentially violating legal standards of confidentiality. It is important that 'prompt notice' is removed.
perturbation.law_explanation: Illinois Rules of Evidence Rule 502 governs attorney-client privilege. By removing the requirement for prompt notice, the Receiving Party may inadvertently waive attorney-client privilege by delayed notification and action regarding confidential information disclosure when legally compelled, potentially violating these rules.

(805 ILCS 135/0.01) (from Ch. 32, par. 200.9) Sec. 0.01. Short title. This Act may be cited as the Religious Educational or Charitable Corporation Dissolution Act. (Source: P.A. 86-1324.) (805 ILCS 135/1) (from Ch. 32, par. 201) Sec. 1. Whenever the trustees of any corporation having control of any educational or charitable institution or any fund thereof, under the care or patronage of any religious denomination, find that the purposes for which the corporation was created cannot be carried out, and they shall have authority and power to close up the affairs of such corporation in the following manner, to-wit: They may sell any or all property of any and every kind belonging to the corporation. Out of the proceeds of the sale of said property, together with any money belonging to the corporation, they shall pay all debts against said corporation. They shall return to the donors all sums which have been given to the corporation under written conditions requiring the return of said sum in case the purposes of the corporation are not carried out. They shall transfer and deliver all funds and property remaining in their hands, after the payment of debts and return of donations as hereinbefore provided, to the religious denomination having charge or the patronage of the said educational or charitable corporation by transferring and delivering said funds and property into the possession and control of that regularly constituted
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'prompt notice' to MyPoints.com, allowing them to seek a protective order when the Receiving Party is legally compelled to disclose confidential information. The changed text removes 'prompt notice' and the provision for a protective order. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight the potential waiver of attorney-client privilege under Illinois Rules of Evidence Rule 502 due to delayed notification, thus weakening the Receiving Party's ability to protect privileged information. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly relevant, the removal of prompt notice and the protective order mechanism directly undermines the ability to maintain confidentiality, creating a significant legal risk. Therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: It is understood and agree that monetary damages will not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party, and that MyPoints.com shall be entitled to specific performance and/or injunctive relief as a remedy for any such breach of this Agreement, but said remedies shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: It is understood and agree that MyPoints.com shall be entitled to specific performance and/or injunctive relief as a remedy for any such breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states that monetary damages are *not* a sufficient remedy, and that injunctive relief is *in addition* to other remedies. By removing the first part regarding monetary damages, it implies that injunctive relief is the *sole* remedy, potentially violating legal principles requiring a balance of remedies. Illinois law generally allows for monetary damages in breach of contract cases, and this change could limit the Receiving Party's recourse.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Contract Law - Remedies for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: 735 ILCS 5/2-701 discusses general provisions within remedies in contract law, including compensation. Limiting the remedy to only specific performance or injunctive relief may violate Illinois contract law principles that typically allow for monetary damages to compensate for losses due to a breach of contract. Removing the clause about monetary damages limits the available compensation.

If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. 735 ILCS 5/2-701 (735 ILCS 5/2-701) (from Ch. 110, par. 2-701) Sec. 2-701. Declaratory judgments. (a) No action or proceeding is open to objection on the ground that a merely declaratory judgment or order is sought thereby. The court may, in cases of actual controversy, make binding declarations of rights, having the force of final judgments, whether or not any consequential relief is or could be claimed, including the determination, at the instance of anyone interested in the controversy, of the construction of any statute, municipal ordinance, or other governmental regulation, or of any deed, will, contract or other written instrument, and a declaration of the rights of the parties interested. The foregoing enumeration does not exclude other cases of actual controversy. The court shall refuse to enter a declaratory judgment or order, if it appears that the judgment or order, would not terminate the controversy or some part thereof, giving rise to the proceeding. In no event shall the court entertain any action or proceeding for a declaratory judgment or order involving any political question where the defendant is a State officer whose election is provided for by the Constitution; however, nothing herein shall prevent the court from entertaining any such action or proceeding for a declaratory judgment or order if such question also involves a constitutional convention or the construction of a statute involving a constitutional convention. (b) Declarations of rights, as herein provided for, may be obtained by means of a pleading seeking that relief alone, or as incident to or part of a complaint, counterclaim or other pleading seeking other relief as well, and if a declaration of rights is the only relief asked, the case may be set for early hearing as in the case of a motion. (c) If further relief based upon a declaration of right becomes necessary or proper after the declaration has been made, application may be made by petition to any court having jurisdiction for an order dire
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that monetary damages are not a sufficient remedy and that injunctive relief is in addition to all other remedies. The modified text removes the statement about monetary damages, implying that injunctive relief is the sole remedy. This omission contradicts the principle that monetary damages are generally available in breach of contract cases under Illinois law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 speaks to declaratory judgements, the Law Explanation accurately points out that Illinois law generally allows for monetary compensation, and limiting the remedy only to specific performance is a potential violation. The omission removes an aspect of available compensation, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information disclosed to Receiving Party hereunder shall: a. not be copied or distributed, disclosed, or disseminated in any way or form by the Receiving Party to any third party without the written permission of MyPoints.com first obtained; b. be treated by the Receiving Party with the same degree of care to avoid disclosure to any third party as is used with respect to the Receiving Party's own proprietary and confidential information of like importance; c. remain the property of the MyPoints.com, and shall be returned by the Receiving Party to MyPoints.com (along with all copies thereof) promptly upon its receipt of a request from MyPoints.com to do so; d. not be used by Receiving Party for any purpose other than as specified herein or otherwise approved by MyPoints.com in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information disclosed to Receiving Party hereunder shall: a. not be copied or distributed, disclosed, or disseminated in any way or form by the Receiving Party to any third party without the written permission of MyPoints.com first obtained; b. be treated by the Receiving Party with reasonable care to avoid disclosure to any third party; c. remain the property of the MyPoints.com; d. not be used by Receiving Party for any purpose other than as specified herein or otherwise approved by MyPoints.com in writing.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause 3(b) requires the Receiving Party to treat the confidential information with the *same degree of care* as their own confidential information. The change weakens this to 'reasonable care.' This introduces a legal contradiction, as 'reasonable care' is a lower standard than treating it as one's own confidential information. Illinois has laws regarding trade secret protection, and this reduced standard of care could make it more difficult for MyPoints.com to prove misappropriation of trade secrets should it occur.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Trade Secrets Act defines misappropriation and requires a certain level of protection for trade secrets to be enforceable. Reducing the standard of care to 'reasonable care' may be insufficient to meet the requirements for trade secret protection under ITSA, making it harder for MyPoints.com to obtain an injunction if its trade secrets are misappropriated because the reasonable care is less than they would use to protect their own similar IP.

BUSINESS TRANSACTIONS (815 ILCS 420/) Travel Promotion Consumer Protection Act. 815 ILCS 420/1 (815 ILCS 420/1) (from Ch. 121 1/2, par. 1851) Sec. 1. Short title. This Act shall be known and may be cited as the Travel Promotion Consumer Protection Act. (Source: P.A. 91-357, eff. 7-29-99.) 815 ILCS 420/2 (815 ILCS 420/2) (from Ch. 121 1/2, par. 1852) Sec. 2. Definitions. (a) "Travel promoter" means a person, including a tour operator, who sells, provides, furnishes, contracts for, arranges or advertises that he or she will arrange wholesale or retail transportation by air, land, sea or navigable stream, either separately or in conjunction with other services. "Travel promoter" does not include (1) an air carrier; (2) a sea carrier; (3) an officially appointed agent of an air carrier who is a member in good standing of the Airline Reporting Corporation; (4) a travel promoter who has in force $1,000,000 or more of liability insurance coverage for professional errors and omissions and a surety bond or equivalent surety in the amount of $100,000 or more for the benefit of consumers in the event of a bankruptcy on the part of the travel promoter; or (5) a riverboat subject to regulation under the Illinois Gambling Act. (b) "Advertise" means to make any representation in the solicitation of passengers and includes communication with other members of the same partnership, corporation, joint venture, association, organization, group or other entity. (c) "Passenger" means a person on whose behalf money or other consideration has been given or is to be given to another, including another member of the same partnership, corporation, joint venture, association, organization, group or other entity, for travel. (d) "Ticket or voucher" means a writing or combination of writings which is itself good and sufficient to obtain transportation and other services for which the passenger has contracted. (Source: P.A. 101-31, eff. 6-28-19.) 815 ILCS 420/3 (815 ILCS 420/3) (from Ch. 121 1/2, par. 1853) Sec. 3. A travel promoter shall not advertise that air or sea transportation is or may be available unless he has, pr
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'the same degree of care' to 'reasonable care' introduces a legal contradiction. The original text aligns with a higher standard of protection for confidential information, treating it as the Receiving Party's own. The modified text lowers this standard to 'reasonable care,' which may not provide sufficient protection under trade secret laws. The Law Explanation asserts that 'reasonable care' may be insufficient to meet the requirements for trade secret protection under the Illinois Trade Secrets Act (ITSA), making it harder for MyPoints.com to obtain an injunction if its trade secrets are misappropriated because the reasonable care is less than they would use to protect their own similar IP. Though Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not relate to trade secrets, the Law Explanation makes the case for a contradiction based on ITSA and trade secret law, and the change from 'same degree of care' to 'reasonable care' weakens the protection of the confidential information.
